notes: Clues written from Wikipedia lead extracts in data/sourcing/docs/religion_mythology_philosophy/. Coverage spans Greek Mythology, Norse/Roman Mythology, Egyptian Mythology, Philosophers, Philosophical Schools, World Religions, and Bible subcategories.

questions.clue: This Titan was chained to a rock and condemned to have his liver consumed by an eagle each day as punishment for bestowing fire upon humanity.

citations.title: Prometheus

questions.clue: This Olympian twin of Artemis served as patron deity of Delphi and was the god through whom the most famous oracle of the ancient world spoke.

citations.title: Apollo

questions.clue: When the three sons of Cronus divided the cosmos by lot after defeating the Titans, this eldest brother drew the underworld as his domain.

citations.title: Hades

questions.clue: This god of the sea and earthquakes carried a trident and was believed to have created horses with a strike of it; his Roman counterpart is Neptune.

citations.title: Poseidon

questions.reviewer_note: Source: poseidon.json — sea god with trident who created horses; Roman equivalent Neptune.

questions.clue: This Norse deity wielded the hammer Mjölnir and was venerated during the Viking Age as a protector of mankind and farmers against giants and chaos.

citations.title: Thor
citations.snippet: Thor is a prominent god in Germanic paganism. In Norse mythology, he is a hammer-wielding god associated with thunder, storms, strength, protection, fertility, and farmers. Besides Old Norse Þórr, the deity occurs in Old English as Thunor,

questions.clue: This Norse shape-shifter once took the form of a mare, was impregnated by a stallion, and gave birth to the eight-legged horse Sleipnir.

questions.reviewer_note: Source: loki.json — shape-shifter who bore Sleipnir while in mare form.

questions.clue: This chief of the Norse Æsir pantheon was accompanied by two ravens named Huginn and Muninn and two wolves, Geri and Freki, and fathered both Baldr and Thor.

citations.title: Odin

questions.reviewer_note: Source: odin.json — head of the Æsir with ravens Huginn and Muninn, wolves Geri and Freki.

questions.clue: This major Egyptian goddess resurrected her slain husband and was considered the divine mother of the pharaoh, whose power was likened to her son Horus.

citations.title: Isis
citations.snippet: Isis was a major goddess in ancient Egyptian religion whose worship spread throughout the Greco-Roman world. Isis was first mentioned in the Old Kingdom as one of the main characters of the Osiris myth, in which she resurrects her slain bro

questions.reviewer_note: Source: isis.json — resurrected Osiris and was the divine mother figure behind the pharaoh.

questions.clue: This jackal-headed deity of ancient Egypt presided over the 'Weighing of the Heart' ceremony, in which a deceased soul's virtue was judged against a feather.

citations.title: Anubis
citations.snippet: Anubis, also known as Inpu, Inpw, Jnpw, or Anpu in Ancient Egyptian, is the god of funerary rites, protector of graves, and guide to the underworld in ancient Egyptian religion, usually depicted as a canine or a man with a canine head.

questions.reviewer_note: Source: anubis.json — jackal-headed deity who presided over the Weighing of the Heart.

questions.clue: This Egyptian god was murdered and dismembered by his own brother Set, then reassembled by his devoted wife to be resurrected and reign over the dead.

citations.title: Osiris

questions.reviewer_note: Source: osiris.json — dismembered by Set, reassembled by Isis, resurrected as ruler of the dead.

questions.clue: This Athenian philosopher wrote nothing himself, yet his method of rigorous questioning gave rise to a whole literary genre of philosophical dialogues recorded by his student Plato.

citations.title: Socrates

questions.clue: This Enlightenment thinker from Königsberg argued in his *Critique of Pure Reason* that space and time are forms of intuition imposed by the human mind, not features of reality itself.

citations.title: Immanuel Kant

questions.clue: Appointed a full professor at age 24, this German thinker spent his later years declaring religion's decline before suffering a complete mental collapse at 44 from which he never recovered.

questions.clue: This ancient philosophical tradition, founded in the Hellenistic period, held that virtue alone is sufficient for happiness and that the universe operates according to reason, called logos.

citations.title: Stoicism
citations.snippet: Stoicism is a philosophical movement and practical guide to living, emphasizing daily self-discipline and moral improvement, which originated in the Hellenistic period of ancient Greece and proliferated well into the Roman Imperial period.

questions.reviewer_note: Source: stoicism.json — Hellenistic school; virtue = happiness; logos governs the universe.

questions.clue: This Indian religion, the world's fourth-largest, teaches that liberation from suffering comes through a Middle Way between extreme asceticism and sensory indulgence.

citations.title: Buddhism
